text.title: arch-pattern-ddd Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for arch-pattern-ddd as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Sharing the same domain model across multiple bounded contexts ("God Context").
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: As the system grows, different teams need to evolve their models independently. A shared model creates deployment coupling and forces coordinated changes across context boundaries.

failure-mode.description: Designing aggregates around database tables rather than business invariants.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Aggregates are consistency boundaries, not persistence units. An aggregate boundary is justified by the invariant it protects. Designing from tables produces anemic aggregates with no real business logic.

failure-mode.description: Making aggregates too large (aggregate includes everything related to an order: customer, items, inventory, payment, shipping).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Large aggregates create contention under concurrency, force large transactions, and make domain events coarse-grained. Keep aggregates small — one to three entities plus value objects is a healthy starting size.

failure-mode.description: Developing the ubiquitous language without domain experts ("developer language").
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Without domain expert input, the model reflects developer intuition, not domain reality. Misleading models produce software that is technically correct but solves the wrong problem.
